You should see two point estimates --- one for `Not enrolled` and one for `Enrolled` --- each with a 95% confidence interval. The gap between them, on the log scale, is the estimated average causal effect of Seguro Popular enrollment.

### Exercise 9

At the R prompt, run `plot_predictions(fit_sps, condition = c("treatment", "sex"))`. CP/CR.

```{r temperance-9}
question_text(NULL,
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = TRUE,
  try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 5)
```

```{r temperance-9-test, include = FALSE}
plot_predictions(fit_sps, condition = c("treatment", "sex"))
```

###

This plot shows predicted log expenditure by both enrollment status and sex. The two lines (or panels) show whether the enrollment effect looks similar for male and female respondents. In a model without an interaction term --- which is what we have --- the lines will be parallel by construction; the model assumes the enrollment effect is the same for men and women.

### Exercise 10

At the R prompt, run `plot_predictions(fit_sps, condition = "treatment", draw = FALSE)`. CP/CR.

```{r temperance-10}
question_text(NULL,
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = TRUE,
  try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 5)
```

```{r temperance-10-test, include = FALSE}
plot_predictions(fit_sps, condition = "treatment", draw = FALSE)
```

###

Because `plot_predictions()` returns a ggplot object, you can continue with ggplot commands like `labs()`. But it can be useful to see the underlying values in the tibble and build your own plot directly.

### Exercise 11

Work with AI to create a beautiful plot starting from the output of `plot_predictions(fit_sps, condition = "treatment", draw = FALSE)`. Do this in your QMD. Title: key variables. Subtitle: the key takeaway about the enrollment effect. Caption: data source. Axis labels: human-readable, noting that the y-axis is on the log scale.

Paste the plot code below.

```{r temperance-11}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    plot_predictions(fit_sps, condition = \"treatment\", draw = FALSE) |>\n      ggplot(aes(x = treatment, y = estimate)) +\n      geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high),\n                      color = \"grey20\", size = 1.1) +\n      labs(\n        title = \"Causal Effect of Seguro Popular Enrollment on Health Spending\",\n        subtitle = \"Enrolled households spent about 24% less than not-enrolled households\",\n        x = \"Enrollment Status\",\n        y = \"Expected Log Health Expenditure (log pesos)\",\n        caption = \"Source: King et al. (2009) via primer.data\"\n      ) +\n      theme_minimal()",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 12)
```

###

Here is our version:

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
plot_predictions(fit_sps, condition = "treatment", draw = FALSE) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = treatment, y = estimate)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high),
                  color = "grey20", size = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Causal Effect of Seguro Popular Enrollment on Health Spending",
    subtitle = "Enrolled households spent about 24% less than not-enrolled households",
    x = "Enrollment Status",
    y = "Expected Log Health Expenditure (log pesos)",
    caption = "Source: King et al. (2009) via primer.data"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

### Exercise 12

Finalize the graphics chunk in your QMD. `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K`. At the R prompt, run `show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")`. CP/CR.

```{r temperance-12}
question_text(NULL,
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = TRUE,
  try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 8)
```

###

The map is not the territory. A beautiful graphic tells a story, but that story is always an imperfect representation of reality.

### Exercise 13

Write the last sentence of your summary paragraph. It describes at least one Quantity of Interest and a measure of uncertainty.

```{r temperance-13}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "We estimate that Seguro Popular enrollment reduced three-month health expenditures by about 24% among positive-spending households (a log-scale coefficient of −0.27, 95% CI approximately −0.62 to 0.08), though the confidence interval includes zero, indicating substantial uncertainty.",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 5)
```

###

Add a final sentence to your summary paragraph. `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K`.

### Exercise 14

Write a few sentences explaining why the estimates might be wrong.

```{r temperance-14}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "The study was conducted in 2005-06 in seven specific Mexican states; applying the results to Mexico in 2025 requires assuming that the relationship between enrollment and spending has not changed over 19 years, which is unlikely given the health system reforms since then. We also restricted our analysis to positive spenders, so the 36% of households that reported zero spending are excluded --- they may have changed their behavior too, just in ways our model cannot see. The confidence interval should probably be wider than the model's nominal range, because neither the temporal stability of the effect nor the representativeness of the 2006 sample is accounted for in the standard errors.",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 8)
```

###

Always go back to your Preceptor Table. Even a perfectly estimated statistical model is rarely as useful as we might like.

### Exercise 15

Rearrange the material in your QMD so the order is graphic, then summary paragraph. The chunk that creates `fit_sps` must occur before the chunk that creates the graphic. `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K`. At the R prompt, run `show_file("analysis.qmd")`. CP/CR.

```{r temperance-15}
question_text(NULL,
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = TRUE,
  try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 10)
```

###

This is the version of your QMD file at which your teacher is most likely to look closely.

### Exercise 16

Publish your rendered QMD to GitHub Pages. In the Terminal (not the R prompt!), run:

```
quarto publish gh-pages analysis.qmd
```

Copy/paste the resulting URL below.

```{r temperance-16}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "https://<your-github-username>.github.io/sps/analysis.html",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 2)
```

###

Commit/push everything.

### Exercise 17

Copy/paste the URL to your GitHub repo.

```{r temperance-17}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "https://github.com/<your-github-username>/sps",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 2)
```

###

We can never know all the entries in the Preceptor Table. That knowledge is reserved for God.

The world confronts us. Make decisions we must.


## Summary

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
plot_predictions(fit_sps, condition = "treatment", draw = FALSE) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = treatment, y = estimate)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high),
                  color = "grey20", size = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Causal Effect of Seguro Popular Enrollment on Health Spending",
    subtitle = "Enrolled households spent about 24% less than not-enrolled households",
    x = "Enrollment Status",
    y = "Expected Log Health Expenditure (log pesos)",
    caption = "Source: King et al. (2009) via primer.data"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

Seguro Popular, Mexico's public health insurance program, aimed to reduce the financial burden of medical care on households. Using data from King et al.'s 2009 randomized evaluation across seven Mexican states, we estimate by what percentage Seguro Popular enrollment reduced out-of-pocket health expenditures among households that were already spending on medical care. After EDA revealed that about 36% of households reported zero spending --- a different question entirely --- we restricted to positive spenders and log-transformed the outcome. The study was conducted in seven specific Mexican states in 2006; whether its results apply to other regions of Mexico today is an open question. We model log household health expenditure as a linear function of Seguro Popular enrollment, age, and sex. We estimate that enrollment reduced three-month health expenditures by about 24% among positive-spending households, though the confidence interval is wide and includes zero.

The world is always more uncertain than our models would have us believe.

```{r download-answers, child = system.file("child_documents/download_answers.Rmd", package = "tutorial.helpers")}
```
